title: RAID Configurations
content:
# OS Interview Prep

## Q1: Why does changing the page table directory pointer during context switches degrade system performance?

### Standard Answer
Changing the page table pointer (e.g., loading CR3 in x86) changes virtual-to-physical address mappings. By default, the CPU page translation cache, the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**, must invalidate its cached mappings because they belong to the previous address space. Consequently, subsequent memory reads trigger slow multi-level page table traversals (TLB misses) until the cache is repopulated. Modern processors mitigate this by tagging TLB entries with Address Space Identifiers (ASID), preventing full flushes.


## Q2: How does the system handle concurrent throughput anomalies under RAID Configurations?

### Standard Answer
The system uses dynamic resource pools and queue-backoff throttles to maintain throughput boundaries, avoiding thread starvation.
